paper_title: Insight into mechanism of divalent metal cations with different d-bands classification in layered double hydroxides for light-driven CO2 reduction

catalyst.source: By varying divalent-metal cations (M Mg 2 , Ni 2 , Cu 2 , Zn 2 ) with different d-bands classification, MAl-LDHs were prepared and inspected for solar-driven CO 2 conversion.
catalyst.context: By varying divalent-metal cations (M Mg 2 , Ni 2 , Cu 2 , Zn 2 ) with different d-bands classification, MAl-LDHs were prepared and inspected for solar-driven CO 2 conversion.The CO and CH 4 yields over these LDHs followed an order of: NiAl-LDHs CuAl-LDHs ZnAl-LDHs MgAl-LDHs.Coupled with multiple characterizations, the activity distinction was revealed from three crucial perspectives: (i) light harvesting, (ii) charge separation and transfer, (iii) surface reactions.The results affirmed that when d orbital was partially occupied with active electrons, both activation of CO 2 and excitation of electrons were much easier to achieve.In-situ DRIFTS manifested that NiAl-LDHs and CuAl-LDHs exhibited more active reaction with CO 2 , which may be related to the upward shifts of the d-band center determined by Valence-band XPS.Particularly, these LDHs exhibited various catalytic stabilities, which was explained by Pearsons hard and soft acid-base (HSAB) principle for the first time

catalyst.source: MAl-LDHs (M Mg, Ni, Cu and Zn) were fabricated via a coprecipitation method coupled with a hydrothermal aging step.
catalyst.context: MAl-LDHs (M Mg, Ni, Cu and Zn) were fabricated via a coprecipitation method coupled with a hydrothermal aging step.Materials and detailed procedures were provided in Supporting Information. The structure of as-prepared samples was identified by Fouriertransform infrared (FTIR, Nicolet IZ10) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D2 PHASER).The morphology of these samples was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S4700) and high- resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F30).The UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS, Shimadzu UV-2600) were measured using BaSO 4 as a reference.X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were calculated on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer.Elemental analysis of samples was analyzed by a Perkin Elmer inductive coupled plasma emission spectrometer (ICP-OES).The texture structure of catalysts was determined by N 2 adsorption-desorption apparatus (Micromeritics ASAP 2460).The surface areas of the samples were calculated by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) methods.In-situ DRIFT experiments were performed on a Nicolet IZ10 spectrometer equipped with a Harrick Scientific DRIFT cell and a NICOLET iZ10 detector.Prior to test, photocatalysts were pretreated at 100 C under N 2 flow (40 mL min) for 1 h and then cooled to 20 C to get the background spectrum.After N 2 flow stopping, CO 2 (40 mLmin) with bubbled H 2 O was introduced into the chamber at 20 C and reacted at dark for 30 min.Then, the light source was turned on to monitor the produced intermediates for another 30 min. Electrochemistry impedance spectroscopy (EIS), photocurrent measurements (i-t curve) and Mott-Schottky (MS) were performed on a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode system.Pt plate and AgAgCl electrode were served as the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively.A 0.5 mol L -1 Na 2 SO 4 solution was used as the electrolyte.The working electrode was prepared as follows: 5 mg catalyst was dissolved in solution containing 30 L Nafion and 1 mL ethyl alcohol.After ultrasonically scattering for 1 h, the slurry was dip-coated onto a FTO glass (1 cm 1 cm) and dried at 80 C for 5 h under a vacuum atmosphere.All the experiments were conducted under a 300 W Xe irradiation. The photoreduction test was conducted in an autoclave with a quartz window at the top (Anhui Kemi Machinery Technology Co., Ltd., China).Prior to the test, photocatalyst (20 mg) and H 2 O (20 mL) were added into the autoclave, and high-purity CO 2 gas was evacuated and refilled several times to get rid of the air.Then, the CO 2 was refilled into the autoclave to reach a pressure of 0.6 MPa and the temperature was controlled at 80 C. A 300 W Xe light with full spectrum irradiation was used as the light source.After irradiation for 10 h, the produced gas was detected and quantified by a gas chromatography (GC 9860) equipped with FID detectors.The computational process of CO and CH 4 production rates were shown in the Supporting Information.

catalyst.source: A series of MAl-LDHs with various the divalent metal cations (M Mg 2 , Ni 2 , Cu 2 , Zn 2 ) were prepared and applied in solar-driven reduction of CO 2 .
catalyst.context: A series of MAl-LDHs with various the divalent metal cations (M Mg 2 , Ni 2 , Cu 2 , Zn 2 ) were prepared and applied in solar-driven reduction of CO 2 .The results affirmed that the divalent metal cations types have great influence on CO yield as follows: NiAl-LDHs (3.5 mol g -1 h -1 ) CuAl-LDHs (3.1 mol g -1 h -1 ) ZnAl-LDHs (2.1 mol g -1 h -1 ) MgAl-LDHs (1.6 mol g -1 h -1 ).NiAl-LDHs and CuAl-LDHs have stronger light harvesting capacity, better charge separation properties and more active surface reactivity with CO 2 relative to MgAl-LDHs and ZnAl-LDHs.According to the in-situ FTIR and valence band XPS measurements, the surface reaction mechanism in LDHs has been proposed.This work clarifies the role of divalent metal cations in LDHs photocatalysts for CO 2 reduction, paving new ways for the development of improved LDHs photocatalyst with high efficiency. Fig. 1.Structural characterizations of MAl-LDHs.(A) FTIR spectra; (B) XRD patterns; XPS spectra of (C) M 1 s or M 2p; (D) O 1 s in the MAl-LDHs and (E) Scheme of LDHs. D 3 ), MgAl-LDHs, NiAl-LDHs and ZnAl-LDHs exhibited thin lamellar structure whilst CuAl-LDHs presented no apparent lamellar structure in accordance with the results in SEM images.All HRTEM showed clear lattice fringes of approximately 0.26 nm, corresponding to (0 1 2) crystal plane of LDHs.The structure characteristic of these samples further confirmed the successful preparation of LDHs, and confirmed that the microstructures of MAl-LDHs were greatly affected by the type of M. Fig. 2. SEM, TEM and HRTEM images of the MAl-LDHs, (A 1 )-(A 4 ) MgAl-LDHs; (B 1 )-(B 4 ) NiAl-LDHs; (C 1 )-(C 4 ) CuAl-LDHs; (D 1 )-(D 4 ) ZnAl-LDHs. Fig. 3. Photocatalytic performance of LDHs.(A) Photocatalytic activity; (B) Time-yield plots; (C) Recycle experiments of MgAl-LDHs; (D) Recycle experiments of NiAl-LDHs; (E) Recycle experiments of CuAl-LDHs; (F) Recycle experiments of ZnAl-LDHs.(Reaction conditions: 80 C, 0.6 MPa, 300 W Xe irradiating for 10 h). Fig. 4. (A) UV-vis DRS spectra; (B) Photocurrent density profiles; (C) EIS Nyquist plots and (D) Measured CB and VB potentials of MAl-LDHs. Fig. 5. (A) Mott-Schottky plots; (B) The pots of (hv) 2 versus energy (hv) and (C) XPS valence band spectra of MAl-LDHs. Fig. 6.In situ FTIR spectra on catalysts (A, E) MgAl-LDHs, (B, F) NiAl-LDHs, (C, G) CuAl-LDHs, and (D, H) ZnAl-LDHs. 60,61, a tentative mechanism for CO and CH 4 formation from CO 2 photoreduction on LDHs was proposed on the basis of in-situ DRIFTS observations, as expressed in reactions (1)-(7) and Fig. 7(C).CO 2 M-O CO 3 2- M-O 2 (1) Fig.7.The 3D colormap surface with projections derived from in situ FTIR spectra for coadsorption of CO 2 and H 2 O vapor on catalysts (A) NiAl-LDHs and (B) CuAl-LDHs; (C) The proposed mechanism of CO 2 conversion on LDHs. Fig. 8. (A) D-band center of the LDHs; (B) Relationships between activity versus the d-band center position for LDHs. , 22 , 34 , 39 , 46 , 60 and 61 (the detail 2 values were showed in Table Physical properties of the as-synthesized LDHs. 2 -was considered as a key intermediate in the photoreduction of CO 2 .Based on theoretical analysis, when one of the C-O bonds in CO 2 -was cleaved to generate C-M bonds, CO was then produced 58.(Reaction 8) If CO 2 -was bonded to H, HCOOH was formed 58.Obviously, our work followed the first mechanism and translated to CO on LDHs surface.CH 4 was another product in this work, requiring multistep reduction.Unfortunately, no obvious stretching vibration of C -H was observed during the 60 min, Band structures for the MAl-LDHs derived from XPS to VB analysis, Mott-Schottky plots and UV-vis DRS measurements. Acknowledgement This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21908108), Jiangsu Province Scientific and Technological Project (BK20180449), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (30920041108), Top-notch Academic Programs Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions, Open Fund for Large Instruments and Equipments of Nanjing University of Science and Technology, A Project by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions. The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https:doi.org10.1016j.cej.2021.130863.

light source.source: By varying divalent-metal cations (M Mg 2 , Ni 2 , Cu 2 , Zn 2 ) with different d-bands classification, MAl-LDHs were prepared and inspected for solar-driven CO 2 conversion.
light source.context: By varying divalent-metal cations (M Mg 2 , Ni 2 , Cu 2 , Zn 2 ) with different d-bands classification, MAl-LDHs were prepared and inspected for solar-driven CO 2 conversion.The CO and CH 4 yields over these LDHs followed an order of: NiAl-LDHs CuAl-LDHs ZnAl-LDHs MgAl-LDHs.Coupled with multiple characterizations, the activity distinction was revealed from three crucial perspectives: (i) light harvesting, (ii) charge separation and transfer, (iii) surface reactions.The results affirmed that when d orbital was partially occupied with active electrons, both activation of CO 2 and excitation of electrons were much easier to achieve.In-situ DRIFTS manifested that NiAl-LDHs and CuAl-LDHs exhibited more active reaction with CO 2 , which may be related to the upward shifts of the d-band center determined by Valence-band XPS.Particularly, these LDHs exhibited various catalytic stabilities, which was explained by Pearsons hard and soft acid-base (HSAB) principle for the first time

light source.source: A 300 W Xe light with full spectrum irradiation was used as the light source.
light source.context: MAl-LDHs (M Mg, Ni, Cu and Zn) were fabricated via a coprecipitation method coupled with a hydrothermal aging step.Materials and detailed procedures were provided in Supporting Information. The structure of as-prepared samples was identified by Fouriertransform infrared (FTIR, Nicolet IZ10) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D2 PHASER).The morphology of these samples was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S4700) and high- resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F30).The UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS, Shimadzu UV-2600) were measured using BaSO 4 as a reference.X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were calculated on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer.Elemental analysis of samples was analyzed by a Perkin Elmer inductive coupled plasma emission spectrometer (ICP-OES).The texture structure of catalysts was determined by N 2 adsorption-desorption apparatus (Micromeritics ASAP 2460).The surface areas of the samples were calculated by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) methods.In-situ DRIFT experiments were performed on a Nicolet IZ10 spectrometer equipped with a Harrick Scientific DRIFT cell and a NICOLET iZ10 detector.Prior to test, photocatalysts were pretreated at 100 C under N 2 flow (40 mL min) for 1 h and then cooled to 20 C to get the background spectrum.After N 2 flow stopping, CO 2 (40 mLmin) with bubbled H 2 O was introduced into the chamber at 20 C and reacted at dark for 30 min.Then, the light source was turned on to monitor the produced intermediates for another 30 min. Electrochemistry impedance spectroscopy (EIS), photocurrent measurements (i-t curve) and Mott-Schottky (MS) were performed on a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode system.Pt plate and AgAgCl electrode were served as the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively.A 0.5 mol L -1 Na 2 SO 4 solution was used as the electrolyte.The working electrode was prepared as follows: 5 mg catalyst was dissolved in solution containing 30 L Nafion and 1 mL ethyl alcohol.After ultrasonically scattering for 1 h, the slurry was dip-coated onto a FTO glass (1 cm 1 cm) and dried at 80 C for 5 h under a vacuum atmosphere.All the experiments were conducted under a 300 W Xe irradiation. The photoreduction test was conducted in an autoclave with a quartz window at the top (Anhui Kemi Machinery Technology Co., Ltd., China).Prior to the test, photocatalyst (20 mg) and H 2 O (20 mL) were added into the autoclave, and high-purity CO 2 gas was evacuated and refilled several times to get rid of the air.Then, the CO 2 was refilled into the autoclave to reach a pressure of 0.6 MPa and the temperature was controlled at 80 C. A 300 W Xe light with full spectrum irradiation was used as the light source.After irradiation for 10 h, the produced gas was detected and quantified by a gas chromatography (GC 9860) equipped with FID detectors.The computational process of CO and CH 4 production rates were shown in the Supporting Information.

light source.source: A series of MAl-LDHs with various the divalent metal cations (M Mg 2 , Ni 2 , Cu 2 , Zn 2 ) were prepared and applied in solar-driven reduction of CO 2 .
light source.context: A series of MAl-LDHs with various the divalent metal cations (M Mg 2 , Ni 2 , Cu 2 , Zn 2 ) were prepared and applied in solar-driven reduction of CO 2 .The results affirmed that the divalent metal cations types have great influence on CO yield as follows: NiAl-LDHs (3.5 mol g -1 h -1 ) CuAl-LDHs (3.1 mol g -1 h -1 ) ZnAl-LDHs (2.1 mol g -1 h -1 ) MgAl-LDHs (1.6 mol g -1 h -1 ).NiAl-LDHs and CuAl-LDHs have stronger light harvesting capacity, better charge separation properties and more active surface reactivity with CO 2 relative to MgAl-LDHs and ZnAl-LDHs.According to the in-situ FTIR and valence band XPS measurements, the surface reaction mechanism in LDHs has been proposed.This work clarifies the role of divalent metal cations in LDHs photocatalysts for CO 2 reduction, paving new ways for the development of improved LDHs photocatalyst with high efficiency. D 3 ), MgAl-LDHs, NiAl-LDHs and ZnAl-LDHs exhibited thin lamellar structure whilst CuAl-LDHs presented no apparent lamellar structure in accordance with the results in SEM images.All HRTEM showed clear lattice fringes of approximately 0.26 nm, corresponding to (0 1 2) crystal plane of LDHs.The structure characteristic of these samples further confirmed the successful preparation of LDHs, and confirmed that the microstructures of MAl-LDHs were greatly affected by the type of M. Fig. 2. SEM, TEM and HRTEM images of the MAl-LDHs, (A 1 )-(A 4 ) MgAl-LDHs; (B 1 )-(B 4 ) NiAl-LDHs; (C 1 )-(C 4 ) CuAl-LDHs; (D 1 )-(D 4 ) ZnAl-LDHs. Fig. 3. Fig. 4. (A) UV-vis DRS spectra; (B) Photocurrent density profiles; (C) EIS Nyquist plots and (D) Measured CB and VB potentials of MAl-LDHs. Fig. 5. (A) Mott-Schottky plots; (B) The pots of (hv) 2 versus energy (hv) and (C) XPS valence band spectra of MAl-LDHs. Fig. 6.In situ FTIR spectra on catalysts (A, E) MgAl-LDHs, (B, F) NiAl-LDHs, (C, G) CuAl-LDHs, and (D, H) ZnAl-LDHs. 60,61, a tentative mechanism for CO and CH 4 formation from CO 2 photoreduction on LDHs was proposed on the basis of in-situ DRIFTS observations, as expressed in reactions (1)-(7) and Fig. 7(C).CO 2 M-O CO 3 2- M-O 2 (1) Fig.7.The 3D colormap surface with projections derived from in situ FTIR spectra for coadsorption of CO 2 and H 2 O vapor on catalysts (A) NiAl-LDHs and (B) CuAl-LDHs; (C) The proposed mechanism of CO 2 conversion on LDHs. Fig. 8. (A) D-band center of the LDHs; (B) Relationships between activity versus the d-band center position for LDHs. , 22 , 34 , 39 , 46 , 60 and 61 (the detail 2 values were showed in Table Physical properties of the as-synthesized LDHs. 2 -was considered as a key intermediate in the photoreduction of CO 2 .Based on theoretical analysis, when one of the C-O bonds in CO 2 -was cleaved to generate C-M bonds, CO was then produced 58.(Reaction 8) If CO 2 -was bonded to H, HCOOH was formed 58.Obviously, our work followed the first mechanism and translated to CO on LDHs surface.CH 4 was another product in this work, requiring multistep reduction.Unfortunately, no obvious stretching vibration of C -H was observed during the 60 min, Band structures for the MAl-LDHs derived from XPS to VB analysis, Mott-Schottky plots and UV-vis DRS measurements. lamp.source: All the experiments were conducted under a 300 W Xe irradiation.
lamp.context: MAl-LDHs (M Mg, Ni, Cu and Zn) were fabricated via a coprecipitation method coupled with a hydrothermal aging step.Materials and detailed procedures were provided in Supporting Information. The structure of as-prepared samples was identified by Fouriertransform infrared (FTIR, Nicolet IZ10) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D2 PHASER).The morphology of these samples was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S4700) and high- resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F30).The UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS, Shimadzu UV-2600) were measured using BaSO 4 as a reference.X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were calculated on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer.Elemental analysis of samples was analyzed by a Perkin Elmer inductive coupled plasma emission spectrometer (ICP-OES).The texture structure of catalysts was determined by N 2 adsorption-desorption apparatus (Micromeritics ASAP 2460).The surface areas of the samples were calculated by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) methods.In-situ DRIFT experiments were performed on a Nicolet IZ10 spectrometer equipped with a Harrick Scientific DRIFT cell and a NICOLET iZ10 detector.Prior to test, photocatalysts were pretreated at 100 C under N 2 flow (40 mL min) for 1 h and then cooled to 20 C to get the background spectrum.After N 2 flow stopping, CO 2 (40 mLmin) with bubbled H 2 O was introduced into the chamber at 20 C and reacted at dark for 30 min.Then, the light source was turned on to monitor the produced intermediates for another 30 min. Electrochemistry impedance spectroscopy (EIS), photocurrent measurements (i-t curve) and Mott-Schottky (MS) were performed on a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode system.Pt plate and AgAgCl electrode were served as the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively.A 0.5 mol L -1 Na 2 SO 4 solution was used as the electrolyte.The working electrode was prepared as follows: 5 mg catalyst was dissolved in solution containing 30 L Nafion and 1 mL ethyl alcohol.After ultrasonically scattering for 1 h, the slurry was dip-coated onto a FTO glass (1 cm 1 cm) and dried at 80 C for 5 h under a vacuum atmosphere.All the experiments were conducted under a 300 W Xe irradiation. The photoreduction test was conducted in an autoclave with a quartz window at the top (Anhui Kemi Machinery Technology Co., Ltd., China).Prior to the test, photocatalyst (20 mg) and H 2 O (20 mL) were added into the autoclave, and high-purity CO 2 gas was evacuated and refilled several times to get rid of the air.Then, the CO 2 was refilled into the autoclave to reach a pressure of 0.6 MPa and the temperature was controlled at 80 C. A 300 W Xe light with full spectrum irradiation was used as the light source.After irradiation for 10 h, the produced gas was detected and quantified by a gas chromatography (GC 9860) equipped with FID detectors.The computational process of CO and CH 4 production rates were shown in the Supporting Information.

reactor type.source: The photoreduction test was conducted in an autoclave with a quartz window at the top (Anhui Kemi Machinery Technology Co., Ltd., China).
reactor type.context: MAl-LDHs (M Mg, Ni, Cu and Zn) were fabricated via a coprecipitation method coupled with a hydrothermal aging step.Materials and detailed procedures were provided in Supporting Information. The structure of as-prepared samples was identified by Fouriertransform infrared (FTIR, Nicolet IZ10) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D2 PHASER).The morphology of these samples was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S4700) and high- resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F30).The UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS, Shimadzu UV-2600) were measured using BaSO 4 as a reference.X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were calculated on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer.Elemental analysis of samples was analyzed by a Perkin Elmer inductive coupled plasma emission spectrometer (ICP-OES).The texture structure of catalysts was determined by N 2 adsorption-desorption apparatus (Micromeritics ASAP 2460).The surface areas of the samples were calculated by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) methods.In-situ DRIFT experiments were performed on a Nicolet IZ10 spectrometer equipped with a Harrick Scientific DRIFT cell and a NICOLET iZ10 detector.Prior to test, photocatalysts were pretreated at 100 C under N 2 flow (40 mL min) for 1 h and then cooled to 20 C to get the background spectrum.After N 2 flow stopping, CO 2 (40 mLmin) with bubbled H 2 O was introduced into the chamber at 20 C and reacted at dark for 30 min.Then, the light source was turned on to monitor the produced intermediates for another 30 min. Electrochemistry impedance spectroscopy (EIS), photocurrent measurements (i-t curve) and Mott-Schottky (MS) were performed on a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode system.Pt plate and AgAgCl electrode were served as the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively.A 0.5 mol L -1 Na 2 SO 4 solution was used as the electrolyte.The working electrode was prepared as follows: 5 mg catalyst was dissolved in solution containing 30 L Nafion and 1 mL ethyl alcohol.After ultrasonically scattering for 1 h, the slurry was dip-coated onto a FTO glass (1 cm 1 cm) and dried at 80 C for 5 h under a vacuum atmosphere.All the experiments were conducted under a 300 W Xe irradiation. The photoreduction test was conducted in an autoclave with a quartz window at the top (Anhui Kemi Machinery Technology Co., Ltd., China).Prior to the test, photocatalyst (20 mg) and H 2 O (20 mL) were added into the autoclave, and high-purity CO 2 gas was evacuated and refilled several times to get rid of the air.Then, the CO 2 was refilled into the autoclave to reach a pressure of 0.6 MPa and the temperature was controlled at 80 C. A 300 W Xe light with full spectrum irradiation was used as the light source.After irradiation for 10 h, the produced gas was detected and quantified by a gas chromatography (GC 9860) equipped with FID detectors.The computational process of CO and CH 4 production rates were shown in the Supporting Information.

operation mode.source: After irradiation for 10 h, the produced gas was detected and quantified by a gas chromatography (GC 9860) equipped with FID detectors.
